note_html: Subjective: HPI: The patient, Larry, a 40-year-old male, has been experiencing chronic back pain for approximately 2 years, which he attributes to moving furniture one weekend. He describes the pain as sharp stabbing in nature, radiating down to his right leg, including his big toe, and worsens with sitting. He also reports numbness and tingling in his right leg, particularly in the foot. He denies any loss of sensation in the genital or rectal area, weakness, or loss of bladder or bowel control. Past medical history: The patient has tried physical therapy and has been prescribed strong pain medications in the past, but with only temporary relief. He has also been considered for surgery, which he wishes to avoid. He has gained weight over the years due to his back pain, which has limited his activity level, including his recent interest in golf. Review of systems: The patient reports difficulty sleeping due to discomfort, but does not mention any other symptoms. Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED] Objective: Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED] Physical exam: The patient has midline tenderness at the L4-L5 disk space with right-sided lumbar paravertebral tenderness. There is pain with lumbar flexion and a positive straight leg test. Decreased sensation to light touch at the right lateral thigh at L4 dermatome. Patellar and Achilles reflexes are symmetrical. Test Results: MRI results show disk herniation with nerve impingement. Assessment / Problem List: Assessment: The patient's symptoms are consistent with an L4-L5 herniated disk with radiculopathy. Problem list: Chronic lumbar back pain; L4-L5 herniated disk with radiculopathy; [NOT MENTIONED] Plan: I recommended a spinal injection with a corticosteroid to reduce inflammation and relieve pain. The procedure will be performed under fluoroscopy to localize the exact area of pain. I will monitor the patient's response to the injection and schedule follow-up appointments as needed, not to exceed four times a year. The procedure is relatively quick and the patient should be out of the office within an hour. Follow-up: The patient will be scheduled for the first injection today, with subsequent follow-ups as needed.
